artifacts.content:
From: dan.whitaker1847@gmail.com
To: emily.carver@carverplanning.com
Subject: Context on the garage studio + a few worries
Date: February 16, 2025

Hey Emily,

Catching up on a quiet Sunday while the kids are building a cardboard “dinosaur museum” in the dining room. I wanted to give you a little more color on why I’m so attached to this garage office/studio setup, in case it helps you understand where I’m coming from.

The big one is work. Ever since that November 2023 outage, when Elena sent the “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability” email and basically put me on that informal PIP, I’ve been hyper-aware of how easily distractions can snowball into real mistakes. That whole thing never went through HR, but it scared me. I don’t want to be the guy who takes down telehealth for half the Midwest because Evan needed Paw Patrol and I lost my train of thought.

On top of that, my anxiety got bad enough last winter that Dr. Michael Jensen at MercyOne Dubuque Family Medicine put me on sertraline. I’ve been doing monthly telehealth check-ins from the garage since February 2024. Having a private, quiet room where I can talk openly without the kids barging in or Anna overhearing every detail has honestly made the meds and therapy stuff way easier.

Money is another reason we’re committed to making this space work. We pulled $45k from the Dupaco Community Credit Union HELOC in June 2024 for the conversion, on top of the ~$18k left from the roof. Anna and I have that “Debt Snowball – Studio + Roof” Google Sheet with the dream of being clear by December 2029, but right now we’re basically just paying interest plus a bit because of childcare and random medical bills. Renting outside office space just isn’t realistic.

Anyway, that’s probably more oversharing than you needed, but it’s the honest picture. Day to day it’s mostly just me on Zoom, Anna painting under those south windows, and the kids running laps in the backyard.

Talk soon,
Dan

metadata.subject: Context on the garage studio + a few worries

artifacts.content:
Title: Work stress + meds – questions for Dr. Jensen (Bluff St. office)
Date: March 4, 2025
Location: Garage office – south window desk

Things to bring up with Dr. Michael Jensen (MercyOne Dubuque Family Medicine) on Friday’s telehealth check-in (3:30 p.m.):

1. Sertraline dosage
- Been on current dose since Feb 2024.
- Anxiety better overall, but spikes around release cycles and those 1:00–3:00 a.m. on-call rotations I’ve been covering since March 2024.
- Ask if it’s normal to feel wired after the late-night incident response shifts in the garage and then useless the next afternoon.

2. Sleep
- On-call schedule from Ravi after the 2023 restructuring: unofficial, but I’m doing some of the telehealth platform pager duty from here in the garage a few nights a month.
- Hard to fall back asleep after a 2 a.m. Zoom from this desk.
- Wondering if he has non-habit-forming ideas (no benzos) that won’t mess with the sertraline.

3. Work stress history
- Mention the November 2023 production outage and Elena’s “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability” email. I still replay that week in my head.
- Ask if my level of catastrophizing about another outage is typical anxiety or veering into something else.

4. Family / caregiving load
- Since Dad’s mild heart attack at UnityPoint Health – Finley Hospital in October 2023 and the stent, I’ve been driving him to cardiology follow-ups every few weeks.
- I keep blocking these on my calendar as “garage office – site inspection” so work doesn’t freak out.
- Ask if it makes sense to look at caregiver support resources, or if that’s more of a therapist thing.

5. Practical
- Confirm it’s okay to keep doing these telehealth visits from the garage office at 1847 Bluff Street (privacy, HIPAA, etc.).
- Ask if he sees any red flags with me taking those evening AWS/security classes through NICC on top of everything.

Remember: don’t minimize stuff. Just say it how it actually feels.

metadata.title: Work stress + meds – questions for Dr. Jensen (Bluff St. office)

artifacts.content:
From: dan.whitaker1847@gmail.com
To: rachel.carlsen@example.com
Subject: Checking in + crazy Bluff Street life
Date: November 20, 2024

Hey Rach,

I’ve been thinking about you since your visit earlier this month and wanted to follow up now that the dust has settled a bit here.

First, I promise I haven’t said a word to Mom or Dad about you and Alex maybe separating. That Signal thread you titled “Rach – Madison stuff (don’t tell Mom & Dad)” is staying between us. If it ever gets to the point where you need a breather, the studio side of the garage here at 1847 Bluff is absolutely yours to crash in for a while. We’ll have to be careful not to make it look like a full-time apartment (no hot plate empire in the corner), but a temporary landing pad is totally fine.

Honestly, we kind of built the space to be flexible. Right now it’s my remote office during the day and Anna’s watercolor cave at night. She’s been out there with her podcasts and painting lights on more lately, which led to our first neighborhood drama: Tom Gallagher from 1843 Bluff Street sent me that late-night text in August — “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block.” I was so annoyed I screenshotted it and saved it in a folder as “Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24.”

I’m trying not to escalate anything with him, because we like the rest of the block and the Washington Neighborhood Association folks have been decent. But it’s a reminder that people are watching what we do back here.

Anyway, you’re not a burden, and if you need to come down from Madison for a week or a month, we’ll make it work. The kids would be thrilled — Lily still talks about the board game marathon you did with them last time.

Love you. Text me on Signal if you want to vent more; Gmail feels too grown-up for that.

Dan

metadata.subject: Checking in + crazy Bluff Street life

artifacts.content:
Title: Dad cardiology follow-up – Finley
Date: April 9, 2025
Time: 2:30–4:30 p.m.
Location: UnityPoint Health – Finley Hospital, Cardiology
Attendees: Me, Dad (Mark)

Notes:
- Pick Dad up at his place by 1:55 p.m. so we have time for parking.
- This is another post–heart attack follow-up after the mild MI he had in October 2023 and the stent placement at Finley.
- Bring list of his meds (he keeps losing the paper one). Ask the cardiologist about:
  * How often they want to see him going forward.
  * Whether the occasional chest tightness he mentioned last Sunday is concerning.
  * Any restrictions on ladder work if he insists on “helping” with house projects.

Work cover:
- Block calendar as “garage office – site inspection” from 2:00–4:30 p.m. so no one at NorthStar wonders why I’m offline.
- Finish code review for telehealth portal tickets before lunch.
- If there’s an incident while I’m gone, Ravi or Jenna can grab the pager; I’ll keep my phone on vibrate in the waiting room but would rather not be doing a 1:00–3:00 a.m.-style incident response from the cardiology lobby.

Personal reminders:
- Ask Dad if he wants to stop by Washington Park afterward for a short walk, weather permitting.
- Maybe bring up the idea of him letting me drive him to more of these instead of Susan doing all the shuttling.
- Don’t mention any of this to Mom beforehand; she’ll just worry more.

From: dan.whitaker1847@gmail.com
To: anna.whitaker@example.com
Subject: Money / studio / summer plans
Date: January 22, 2025

Hey love,

I was looking at the “Debt Snowball – Studio + Roof” sheet over lunch and wanted to jot some thoughts down before I forget them.

We’re sitting at around $63k on the Dupaco HELOC now — the original ~$18k from the roof plus the $45k we pulled in June 2024 for the garage conversion. If we keep doing just interest + a little extra, we’re never going to hit that December 2029 goal we wrote in the notes. I get why we’re stuck there (childcare, Lily’s dentist bill, Evan’s ear infection saga, etc.), but it’s weighing on me.

The flip side is that the studio really is pulling its weight. My days in the garage office feel 100x saner than when I was at the dining room table. Even with those random 1:00–3:00 a.m. on-call nights Ravi added after the 2023 restructuring, at least I’m not waking you and the kids up — it’s just me, the laptop, and Jasper snoring on the couch.

It also doubles as my classroom. Those NICC AWS and security courses I’ve been taking in the evenings feel like an insurance policy if NorthStar decides to cut more remote roles. That Canvas note I pinned — “If NSHT cuts remote roles – exit plan 2025” — sounds dramatic, but it helps to have a plan.

Maybe when your subbing schedule calms down we can sit down with the budget and see if there’s anywhere to squeeze out an extra $150–200/month toward principal. Even that would make the spreadsheet graph look less terrifying.

Unrelated: can we try to keep the painting sessions wrapped by 9:30 p.m. on weeknights? I’m still a little paranoid about another “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block” text from Tom.

See you in a bit for kid-wrangling.

Dan

Thread: Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24 (saved screenshot)
Date: August 14–15, 2024
Participants: Dan (me), Anna

[8/14/24, 10:41 p.m. – Screenshot description]

Text from Tom Gallagher (1843 Bluff):
Tom: “your wife’s painting lights in the garage are on past 10 again – this is a quiet block”

My reply to Tom:
Me: “Hey Tom, thanks for letting us know. Anna’s just wrapping up now. We’ll be more mindful about lights after 10.”

[Forwarded to Anna]

Me: “FYI, this just came in from Tom next door. I took a screenshot and labeled it ‘Tom – studio complaint 8/14/24’ so I don’t forget the wording.”

Anna: “Wow. It’s 10:40, not midnight. I was literally rinsing brushes.”

Me: “I know. But given how twitchy people can be about the studio, I’m trying to keep a paper trail in case anyone starts saying we’re running some wild operation back there.”

Anna: “Ugh. Do you think this is about your late-night on-call stuff too? You’re out there for those 1–3 a.m. telehealth incidents sometimes.”

Me: “Doubt he even notices that. When I’m doing those shifts from the garage it’s just me and a laptop with headphones. No louder than you listening to podcasts while you paint.”

Anna: “Still feels like he’s watching us. Maybe I should set an alarm for 9:55 so I’m definitely done by 10 on school nights.”

Me: “That would help. I’ll also make sure the blinds are down when I’m on my sertraline check-ins with Dr. Jensen so we’re not giving anyone a show.”

Anna: “Cool. I just don’t want this turning into neighborhood drama. I like most of these people.”

Me: “Same. We’ll keep it boring and neighborly. Now come inside, it’s bedtime.”

Title: Career / remote work brain dump – 1847 Bluff studio
Date: May 12, 2025
Location: Garage office couch

Just need to get this out of my head.

Work feels both stable and fragile. On paper, I’m a Senior Software Engineer at NorthStar Health Technologies, fully remote, living this ideal “walk across the yard to the garage office” life. In reality, I still hear Elena’s voice from that November 2023 week after the production outage, when she sent the “Next 60 days – expectations for portal stability” message and pulled me aside for the quasi-PIP talk. No HR record, but it lives rent-free in my brain.

Since the 2023 restructuring, Ravi has us absorbing late-night on-call for the telehealth platform. I’ve been quietly doing some of those 1:00–3:00 a.m. incident response shifts from this couch, even though my official schedule says 8–5. It’s not noisy — just me, logs, and alerts — but it adds to the feeling that everything could fall apart if I miss one alert.

That’s part of why the NICC AWS and security classes matter so much. Logging into Canvas from the south-window desk at 9 p.m., pulling up that note titled “If NSHT cuts remote roles – exit plan 2025,” makes me feel like I have some agency. The garage is my office, but it’s also my backup plan incubator.

Meanwhile, Dad’s heart stuff and the Finley Hospital cardiology runs keep reminding me that time is finite. I block those appointments as “garage office – site inspection” on my work calendar so nobody asks questions, then sit in the waiting room refreshing Slack like a weirdo.

I don’t know if the answer is to double down at NorthStar, polish the résumé, or just keep quietly skilling up out here in the Bluff Street studio while the kids sleep. For now, this space is the one piece that feels like it’s actually under my control.

metadata.title: Career / remote work brain dump – 1847 Bluff studio

artifacts.content:
Hey Emily,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to catch up on a few things regarding our home on Bluff Street and some plans we're considering for the future.

First off, I’ve signed off on the property owners' certification today (2025-02-10) as we discussed. I appreciate all the help you’ve provided so far. I noticed the legal description is quite detailed: Lot 12 and the North 10 feet of Lot 13, Block 7, City of Dubuque, Iowa, according to the Original Plat thereof. The parcel ID is 10-03-31-234-012, and the total property lot area is 7,850 sq ft (0.18 acres), which gives us a nice space to work with.

Speaking of which, you mentioned Carver Planning & Design is just around the corner at 920 Main Street, Suite 305. It’s great to have you so close by here in Dubuque, IA 52001. If I need to reach you, I’ll use your office phone at (563) 555-7402 or your cell at (563) 555-7410.

Anna and I have been thinking a lot about how to best utilize our space without getting caught up in any major zoning changes or historic designations. Thankfully, our existing zoning is R-2 Two-Family Residential, which keeps things straightforward. We’re not looking for any variances, subdivisions, or text amendments at this time. Also, we’re steering clear of any historic housing grants or landmark statuses for now. The last thing we want is to get tangled up in demolition or rezoning issues, so we're keeping things simple.

As you know, our neighborhood doesn't have any special exceptions or temporary use permits in play, which is a relief. We can focus on maintaining the home’s character and making those incremental improvements we talked about, like finishing the kids’ shared bedroom and restoring some of the original trim work.

If anything changes on your end or if you have new ideas that might fit our goals, please let me know. Thanks again for your continued support and guidance!

Best,
Daniel R. Whitaker

P.S. If you need to reach me, my phone number is still (563) 417-8294, but you already knew that!

metadata.subject: Updates on Bluff Street and Future Plans
